name: Public Procurement Act
description: Provisions of Public Procurement Act 2007: objectives, procedures, methods, thresholds, penalties.

subcategories.name: Objectives & Institutions
subcategories.description: Procurement objectives, institutions, and governance architecture.

questions.question: In which year was the Public Procurement Act enacted in Nigeria
questions.explanation: The Public Procurement Act was enacted in 2007 to regulate public procurement practices in Nigeria.

questions.question: Which body is established by the Public Procurement Act to oversee procurement in Nigeria
questions.explanation: The Bureau of Public Procurement (BPP) is established under the Act as the regulatory authority for procurement.

questions.question: Which section of the **PPA** 2007 establishes the Bureau of Public Procurement
questions.explanation: Section 3 of the PPA 2007 legally establishes the Bureau of Public Procurement (BPP).

questions.question: Which of the following is **NOT** an objective of the Public Procurement Act
questions.explanation: The Act seeks to promote transparency, competition, efficiency, and value for money, explicitly prohibiting corruption.

questions.question: Which section empowers the BPP to issue guidelines and regulations to **MDAs**'
questions.explanation: Section 5 gives BPP the power to issue guidelines, manuals, and regulations for standardized procurement.

questions.question: Which section requires all procuring entities to establish a Procurement Planning Committee (PPC)
questions.explanation: Section 21 requires every procuring entity to have a Procurement Planning Committee,.

questions.question: Who statutorily chairs the Procurement Planning Committee of a Ministry
questions.explanation: The Procurement Planning Committee is chaired by the Permanent Secretary or Chief Executive Officer (the Accounting Officer) of the procuring entity.

questions.question: What is the primary role of the Procurement Unit in an MDA?
options: Prepare bidding documents and manage procurement processes.
Authorize payments in the evaluation stage.
questions.explanation: The procurement unit prepares bidding documents and manages the procurement process for the MDA.

questions.question: Which section provides for the establishment of procurement journals to publish notices
questions.explanation: Section 12 mandates the publication of procurement journals to ensure wide dissemination of information.

questions.question: Which section empowers the BPP to maintain and update a national database of contractors and service providers
questions.explanation: Sections 5 and 9 empower the BPP to maintain and update a national database of eligible contractors Reference: section empowers.

questions.question: The power to organize and carry out training for procurement officers falls under which BPP power
questions.explanation: Section 5 empowers the BPP to organize training and build capacity of procurement officers.

questions.question: Which section empowers the BPP to issue Certificates of 'No Objection' to contract awards above designated thresholds
questions.explanation: Section 5 mandates BPP to issue a 'Certificate of No Objection' before high-value contract awards can be finalized.

questions.question: Which section empowers the BPP to inspect procurement records and documents of entities to ensure compliance
questions.explanation: Section 5 empowers the BPP to inspect procurement records to ensure compliance.

questions.question: Which section covers the conduct of mandatory procurement audits by the BPP
questions.explanation: Section 6 empowers BPP to conduct procurement audits of procuring entities.

questions.question: The primary objective of the Public Procurement Act, 2007 is to ensure:
options: Transparency, accountability, and value for money.
questions.explanation: The Act's goal is to ensure transparency, accountability, and value for money in public expenditure.

questions.question: Which section prohibits a procuring entity from initiating procurement without approved budgetary provision and availability of funds
questions.explanation: Section 14 strictly prohibits procurement without approved budgetary provision,.

questions.question: The principle that mandates **MDAs** to seek the best combination of cost and quality/specifications is known as:
questions.explanation: Section 16 emphasizes that all procurement must achieve optimum value for money.

questions.question: The Act prohibits the illegal breaking down of a contract to circumvent approval thresholds, a practice known as:
questions.explanation: Section 16 (and Section 58(4)) explicitly prohibits the splitting of contracts to evade approval thresholds.

questions.question: Which procurement method is designated as the default and preferred approach to ensure fair competition
questions.explanation: Open Competitive Bidding is the mandatory default procurement method under Section 24 of the PPA 2007,.

questions.question: When is **Direct Procurement (Sole Sourcing)** generally permitted under the **PPA**'
options: When the Accounting Officer prefers a specific vendor.
Only in cases of extreme urgency (emergency) or single source availability.
questions.explanation: Direct Procurement (Section 42) is permitted only for emergencies or proprietary purchases where only one known source exists,.

questions.question: When is restricted tendering justified under the PPA?
options: Availability from only a few qualified suppliers.
Preference of the Accounting Officer for a smaller field.
questions.explanation: Restricted tendering is justified where only a limited number of qualified suppliers are available.

questions.question: The principle of **Due Process** in public procurement mandates:
options: Contract award to the cheapest bidder regardless of quality.
Strict adherence to all legal and procedural requirements of the PPA.
The inclusion of a military representative in the Tenders Board.
Exclusive use of Sole Sourcing methods.
questions.explanation: Due Process emphasizes strict compliance with legal, procedural, and administrative requirements throughout the contract process.

questions.question: Procurement involving supplies, materials, equipment, and tangible supplies falls under which **PPA** classification
questions.explanation: The PPA classifies procurement into Goods (tangible supplies), Works (construction), and Services (advisory/operational).

questions.question: Procurement covering the construction, rehabilitation, or physical improvement of infrastructure falls under which **PPA** classification
questions.explanation: Works are classified as infrastructure projects (construction, rehabilitation, or improvement of physical assets).

questions.question: The acquisition of specialized intellectual or advisory tasks (e.g., strategic consulting) is classified under:
questions.explanation: Consultancy Services involve intellectual or advisory tasks and require specific selection methods (Section 43).

questions.question: Who is ultimately responsible for ensuring all financial commitments, including procurement, comply with the **PPA** and budgetary limits within an **MDA**'
options: The Head of the Civil Service of the Federation (OHCSF).
questions.explanation: The Permanent Secretary/CEO, as the Chief Accounting Officer, holds ultimate personal responsibility for expenditure control and compliance,.

questions.question: Before an **MDA** commences procurement planning, the PPC must establish the justification and scope through a:
questions.explanation: Needs Assessment precedes planning (Section 21) and determines the project scope, technical specifications, and quantities required, ensuring alignment with the budget.

questions.question: Procurement documents and records must be retained for audit and inspection purposes for a minimum period of:
questions.explanation: Procurement documents must be retained for at least 5 years (Section 38) to support potential audits and inquiries,.

questions.question: The use of **International Competitive Bidding (ICB)** is sanctioned under which Section of the **PPA**'
questions.explanation: Section 27 provides for International Competitive Bidding when national competitive bidding is inadequate or the required goods/services are unavailable locally.

questions.question: Which body is responsible for setting and publishing the actual monetary threshold limits (e.g., ₦50m, ₦100m) for **MDAs**' internal approval boards
questions.explanation: The BPP establishes and publishes the Threshold Manual, setting approval limits for Accounting Officers and Tender Boards.

questions.question: The rule that allows for preferential scoring or price advantage for local firms to encourage the use of Nigerian goods and services is known as:
questions.explanation: Section 35 allows for Domestic Preference in procurement to support local industry.

questions.question: Procurement undertaken to address unexpected events like disasters or national security threats, where rapid action is mandatory, is called:
questions.explanation: Emergency Procurement (Section 42) is reserved for unforeseen events where competitive delays would compromise life or security.

questions.question: What is the mandatory administrative action required immediately after concluding an Emergency Procurement (**Section 42**)
options: Publicizing the award in the National Gazette.
Submitting a detailed report detailing the circumstances and rationale to the BPP for ratification.
questions.explanation: Emergency procurement must be subject to immediate audit and ratification by submitting a report detailing the necessity to the BPP Reference: Section 42).

questions.question: Which section provides for the use of a **Framework Agreement** for repeated acquisitions of commonly used goods or services over a specified period
questions.explanation: Section 44 provides for Framework Agreements for repeated procurements under pre-agreed terms.

questions.question: Which section of the **PPA** mandates that procuring entities must treat all bidders fairly and equitably
questions.explanation: Section 16 requires equal and fair treatment of all bidders during the procurement process.

questions.question: What is the role of the National Council on Public Procurement (NCPP)
options: To award all contracts above ₦5 billion.
To resolve industrial disputes in the public service.
To provide high-level policy direction and oversight to the BPP.
To manage the Federal Government's Treasury Single Account (TSA).
questions.explanation: The NCPP provides policy direction and high-level oversight of the BPP, reporting directly to the President.

questions.question: Which procurement method must be used if competition is limited to a few suppliers but price quotations are obtained from them, requiring BPP approval
questions.explanation: Restricted Tendering (Section 40) is the method that invites a limited, pre-qualified group of vendors to compete.

questions.question: Procurement planning is essential because it ensures the **MDA** avoids:
questions.explanation: Proper planning is critical to align needs with budget (Section 14) and prevent illegal Contract Splitting (Section 16).

questions.question: Procurement that requires advice on organizational restructuring or technical feasibility falls under which category?
questions.explanation: Advisory and intellectual tasks such as restructuring advice and feasibility studies are classified as Consultancy Services under the PPA.

questions.question: Which section requires procuring entities to publish advertisements for all procurements above specified thresholds in national newspapers
questions.explanation: Section 23 requires mandatory advertisement of procurement opportunities above thresholds to ensure transparency,.

questions.question: What is the minimum statutory advertisement period required for bidding opportunities under national competitive bidding
questions.explanation: Section 25 requires at least 4 weeks for submission of bids under national competitive bidding.

questions.question: The procurement objective that encourages community participation in small-scale, localized projects is provided for in:
questions.explanation: Section 39 provides for community participation in small-scale procurement to encourage local development.

questions.question: What is the likely consequence if a procuring entity fails to ensure due process and transparency?
options: The contract automatically defaults to the cheapest bidder.
The Accounting Officer may face disciplinary action, including dismissal or surcharge.
The National Assembly must disband the MDA.
The BPP must issue a commendation.
questions.explanation: A failure to ensure due process and transparency can trigger disciplinary sanctions against the Accounting Officer.

questions.question: The Ministerial Tenders Board (MTB) has the authority to approve contracts that fall **below** the ceiling set by the:
questions.explanation: Contracts exceeding the MTB threshold must be reviewed and approved by the Federal Executive Council (FEC),.

questions.question: The BPP’s mandate to maintain a national database of contractors (**Section 9**) primarily supports which procurement objective
options: Tracking contractor performance and verifying eligibility/capacity.
questions.explanation: The database monitors performance and facilitates sanctions like blacklisting, while ensuring bidders meet eligibility requirements (Section 16).

questions.question: Which term describes the sequential documentation of financial and procurement transactions, ensuring integrity and traceability for verification
questions.explanation: The Audit Trail provides a chronological record of transactions, vital for accountability and traceability, which is supported by the 5-year record retention rule.

questions.question: What are the three main classes of procurement under the Public Procurement Act?
questions.explanation: The Public Procurement Act classifies procurement under three main heads: Goods, Works, and Services. The correct option therefore names those three classes.

questions.question: For small value purchases below the competitive threshold, strict financial regulations typically mandate that the **MDA** must obtain a minimum of:
questions.explanation: Although the PPA favors open bidding, supplementary financial guidelines require at least three quotations for small purchases to ensure basic value for money.

questions.question: What is the statutory penalty for a procurement officer found guilty of violating the **PPA**, such as abuse of office or contract splitting
options: A promotion to a non-procurement post.
Procedurally, demotion or reduction in rank within regulatory requirements.
In procurement practice, imprisonment and/or a substantial fine in authorized evaluation process.
Within public procurement workflow, mandatory annual leave under due process safeguards.
questions.explanation: Section 58 prescribes imprisonment and fines for serious procurement-related fraud and breaches by officials and contractors.

questions.question: The power of the BPP to cancel a procurement proceeding before contract award if irregularities are found (**Section 53**) reinforces the principle of:
questions.explanation: Section 53 empowers the BPP to suspend or cancel proceedings, directly enforcing Due Process and holding the MDA accountable for irregularities.

questions.question: The Nigerian Public Procurement Act (**PPA**) strategically emphasizes **Open Competitive Bidding** as the default method to promote:
options: Fairness, competition, and value for money.
questions.explanation: Open Competitive Bidding is the default method (PPA Section 24) to ensure fairness, competition, efficiency, and value for money,.

questions.question: Which section empowers the BPP to issue a 'Certificate of No Objection' before a contract award can be finalized
questions.explanation: Section 5 mandates BPP to issue a 'Certificate of No Objection' for high-value procurements.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of holding a public bid opening ceremony
options: To announce bid prices and ensure transparency.
questions.explanation: Bid opening ensures transparency by publicly announcing bid prices and bidders' names Reference: PPA – Bid Opening Purpose.

questions.question: Which action best demonstrates sound objectives and institutions governance?
options: Compliance with approved procedures supported by complete records.
Inconsistent application of rules across similar cases.
Bypassing of review checkpoints during implementation.
Preference for convenience over procurement compliance.
questions.explanation: Sound governance in procurement objectives and institutions work depends on approved procedure and complete records that support oversight.

questions.question: For effective objectives & institutions, what is the most appropriate approach to ensure objectives & institutions compliance
options: Use lawful criteria and document each decision step transparently.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
questions.explanation: Use lawful criteria and document each decision step transparently. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in objectives & institutions Reference: Objectives & Institutions - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which action best supports risk management in objectives and institutions work?
options: Early identification of control gaps with prompt escalation of material exceptions.
questions.explanation: Risk management starts with early identification of gaps and prompt escalation of important exceptions.

questions.question: When handling objectives & institutions, which choice reflects proper open competition standards
options: Use competitive procurement methods except where lawful exceptions apply.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
questions.explanation: Use competitive procurement methods except where lawful exceptions apply. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in objectives & institutions Reference: Objectives & Institutions - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice should a responsible officer prioritize to sustain bid evaluation in Objectives & Institutions
options: Apply published criteria consistently to all responsive bids.
Prioritize convenience over policy and legal requirements.
Ignore feedback and continue non-compliant procedures.
Apply rules inconsistently based on personal preference.
questions.explanation: Apply published criteria consistently to all responsive bids. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in objectives & institutions Reference: Objectives & Institutions - Expansion Set.

questions.question: In the context of Objectives & Institutions, which action best demonstrates contract governance
options: Monitor delivery milestones and enforce contract obligations.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
questions.explanation: Monitor delivery milestones and enforce contract obligations. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in objectives & institutions Reference: Objectives & Institutions - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice best supports procurement ethics in objectives and institutions work?
options: Prevention of collusion, favoritism, and conflicts of interest in consultant selection.
Inconsistent application of rules across similar cases.
Bypassing of review checkpoints during implementation.
questions.explanation: Ethical objectives-and-institutions work requires preventing collusion, favoritism, and conflicts of interest in consultant selection and related procurement activity.

questions.question: Which option most strongly aligns with good public-service practice on regulatory compliance within Objectives & Institutions
options: Obtain required approvals before award and contract execution.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
questions.explanation: Obtain required approvals before award and contract execution. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in objectives & institutions Reference: Objectives & Institutions - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice best supports documented procedure in objectives and institutions work?
options: Recording of each decision step with preserved file evidence.
Inconsistent application of rules across similar cases.
Bypassing of review checkpoints during implementation.
Preference for convenience over procurement compliance.
questions.explanation: Documented procedure is preserved when each decision step is recorded and the supporting file evidence is kept for later verification.

questions.question: Which practice should a responsible officer prioritize to sustain compliance assurance in Objectives & Institutions
options: Apply approved rules consistently and escalate exceptions.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
questions.explanation: Apply approved rules consistently and escalate exceptions. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in objectives & institutions Reference: Objectives & Institutions - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which action best demonstrates public accountability in objectives and institutions work?
options: Traceable decisions supported by evidence-based justification.
Bypassing of review checkpoints during implementation.
Preference for convenience over procurement compliance.
questions.explanation: Public accountability in procurement institutions work depends on decisions that are traceable and supported by evidence-based reasons.

questions.question: For effective objectives & institutions, what is the most appropriate approach to ensure service integrity
options: Avoid conflicts of interest and disclose relevant constraints.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
questions.explanation: Avoid conflicts of interest and disclose relevant constraints. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in objectives & institutions Reference: Objectives & Institutions - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice best supports risk control in objectives and institutions work?
options: Early identification of risk with applied safeguards and documented mitigation.
questions.explanation: Risk control means spotting risks early, applying controls, and documenting how they were managed.

questions.question: When handling objectives & institutions, which choice reflects proper decision transparency standards
options: Use clear criteria and communicate decisions promptly.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
questions.explanation: Use clear criteria and communicate decisions promptly. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in objectives & institutions Reference: Objectives & Institutions - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice best sustains operational discipline in objectives and institutions work?
options: Approved workflows with verification of outputs before closure.
questions.explanation: Operational discipline means following the approved workflow and checking the output before closure.

questions.question: In the context of Objectives & Institutions, which action best demonstrates citizen-focused service
options: Balance legality, fairness, timeliness, and service quality.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
questions.explanation: Balance legality, fairness, timeliness, and service quality. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in objectives & institutions Reference: Objectives & Institutions - Expansion Set.

options: Set measurable targets, monitor progress, and correct deviations.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
questions.explanation: Set measurable targets, monitor progress, and correct deviations. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in objectives & institutions Reference: Objectives & Institutions - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which action best demonstrates objectives and institutions governance?
options: Maintenance of an auditable trail for every decision.
questions.explanation: A good governance trail shows how each decision was made and recorded.

options: Use lawful criteria and document each decision step transparently.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
questions.explanation: Use lawful criteria and document each decision step transparently. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in objectives & institutions Reference: Objectives & Institutions - Expansion Set.

Prioritize convenience over policy and legal requirements.

Apply rules inconsistently based on personal preference.
questions.explanation: Identify control gaps early and escalate material exceptions promptly. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in objectives & institutions Reference: Objectives & Institutions - Expansion Set.

options: Use competitive procurement methods except where lawful exceptions apply.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
questions.explanation: Use competitive procurement methods except where lawful exceptions apply. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in objectives & institutions Reference: Objectives & Institutions - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which body is expressly mentioned as part of the Public Service apart from the Civil Service?
questions.explanation: Correct option: A (Armed Forces). Apart from the Civil Service, the Public Service also encompasses the Armed Forces, the Judiciary, the Legislature and the Police..

questions.question: What is a critical success factor for government to achieve its objectives?
options: A professional, competent, and result-oriented Civil Service.
questions.explanation: A professional, competent, and result-oriented Civil Service is a critical success factor for government to achieve its objectives. The item is therefore testing recognition of that governance requirement.

questions.question: At the start of a procurement committee meeting, what practice best reinforces seriousness and order?
options: Open the meeting on time and restate the agenda clearly.
Delay the meeting until informal discussions end.
Start decisions before confirming the agenda.
Treat punctuality as optional for senior members.
questions.explanation: A timely start and a clear restatement of the agenda establish order and seriousness at the meeting.

questions.question: What minimum amount on a receipt for goods supplied or services rendered attracts a N50 stamp duty?
questions.explanation: Financial Regulation 620(a)(i) states that a receipt for goods supplied or services rendered attracts a N50 stamp duty when the amount acknowledged is N1,000 or more. The threshold being tested is therefore N1,000.

questions.question: Which of the following is identified as a core principle in the discharge of Civil Service functions?
questions.explanation: Responsiveness is identified as one of the principles guiding the discharge of Civil Service functions, alongside accountability, courtesy, objectivity, impartiality, and public trust. The correct option therefore points to responsiveness, not secrecy or partisanship.

questions.question: What is the effect of using a nominee, trustee, or agent to carry out a prohibited act?
options: Continuing treatment as a breach of the Code.
Automatic absolution of the principal offender.
Disregard of the act because it was not done personally.
Treatment of the act as official duty.
questions.explanation: Using a nominee, trustee, or agent does not remove responsibility; the act still breaches the Code.

questions.question: Which institution is expressly mentioned as part of the Public Service apart from the Civil Service?
questions.explanation: Correct option: A (Armed Forces). (Armed Forces). Apart from the Civil Service, the Public Service also encompasses the Armed Forces, the Judiciary, the Legislature and the Police..

questions.question: Which factor is identified as critical for government to achieve its objectives?
options: A professional, competent, and result-oriented Civil Service.
questions.explanation: A professional, competent, and result-oriented Civil Service is regarded as a critical success factor for government to achieve its objectives. The item therefore tests recognition of that public-administration requirement.

questions.question: According to guidance on irrelevance in official writing, what should officers avoid?
options: Having a grasp of the subject at issue.
Drifting away from the question under consideration.
questions.explanation: Guidance on irrelevance in official writing warns officers not to drift away from the question under consideration.

questions.question: which option correctly addresses: If a payment to a firm is in doubt due to loss of relevant documents and an initial investigation reveals no prior payment, what must the payee issue to indemnify the federal government against double payment?
questions.explanation: Correct option: C (A certificate of indemnity.). Financial Regulation 618 states: "...the payee must be made to issue a certificate of indemnity to indemnify the federal government against double disbursement should an earlier disbursement be established at a later date."

questions.question: Which description best reflects the principle of diligence expected of a Civil Servant?
options: To work only during official hours and do no more.
To be hardworking, conscientious, and committed to giving their best to the job.
To delegate all tasks to subordinates regardless of responsibility.
questions.explanation: The principle of diligence requires a Civil Servant to be hardworking, conscientious, and committed to giving their best to official duties.

questions.question: What is a central aim of the Official Secrets Act?
options: To allow a free flow of information to the public.
To protect civil servants from prosecution.
To prevent the leaking of government secrets.
To encourage civil servants to disclose all government information.
questions.explanation: A central aim of the Official Secrets Act is to prevent the leakage of government secrets to unauthorized persons.

questions.question: Which database does the Bureau of Public Procurement maintain to support transparency in public procurement?
options: National database of contractors and service providers.
National database of standard workflow prices.
questions.explanation: Section 10 of the Public Procurement Act empowers the BPP to maintain and update a national database of contractors and service providers to support transparency and planning.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the BPP's national database of contractors and service providers?
options: To support procurement planning and oversight.
To issue salary payments to civil servants.
questions.explanation: The database helps the BPP and MDAs track suppliers and improve procurement oversight, transparency, and planning.

subcategories.name: Bidding, Evaluation & Award
subcategories.description: Bidding rules, evaluation methods, and award procedures.

questions.question: Which section of the **PPA** 2007 requires public advertisement of procurement opportunities above specified thresholds
questions.explanation: Section 23 requires the advertisement of all procurements above the set thresholds to ensure transparency [1-3].

questions.question: What is the minimum statutory time frame required for the submission of bids under National Competitive Bidding (NCB)
questions.explanation: Section 25 requires at least 4 weeks (28 days) for the submission of bids under national competitive bidding.

questions.question: All standard bidding documents (SBDs) used by **MDAs** must be based on templates issued by which body
options: Office of the Head of Civil Service of the Federation (OHCSF).
questions.explanation: Section 25 requires procuring entities to use standard bidding documents containing clear specifications and criteria, typically issued by the BPP.

questions.question: The process of verifying a bidder's technical capacity and qualifications *before* they are invited to submit bids is known as:
questions.explanation: Section 22 provides for pre-qualification of bidders to ensure capability before the formal submission of bids.

questions.question: According to the **PPA**, which financial document must bidders submit as a mandatory eligibility requirement, typically covering the preceding three years
questions.explanation: Section 16 requires bidders to have fulfilled all tax obligations, necessitating the submission of a valid Tax Clearance Certificate (TCC).

questions.question: Under **Section 16**, which condition disqualifies a contractor from being considered an eligible bidder
options: Having been in operation for less than one year.
Being in receivership, bankruptcy, or insolvency.
Using sub-contractors for part of the work.
questions.explanation: Section 16 states that eligible contractors must not be in receivership, bankruptcy, or insolvency.

questions.question: What is the primary function of a **Bid Security**'
options: To guarantee the contractor's performance after award.
To prevent bidders from withdrawing their tender before the expiration of the validity period.
To cover the government's cost of preparing bidding documents.
To serve as the mobilization fee.
questions.explanation: Section 28 requires bid security to protect procuring entities from default, such as withdrawal or failure to sign a contract, by bidders.

questions.question: What is the typical validity period for bids, unless otherwise specified in the tender documents
questions.explanation: Section 26 provides that bids shall remain valid for 60 days unless otherwise stated.

questions.question: The Bid Opening ceremony must be conducted in public to ensure transparency, as required by which section of the **PPA**'
questions.explanation: Section 27 requires that bid opening must be done publicly to ensure fairness and transparency.

questions.question: Immediately following a public bid opening, which documents must be recorded in the official minutes
options: The BPP's final No Objection Certificate.
The names of all bidders and the bid prices.
The political affiliation of the bidders.
questions.explanation: Bid opening minutes must record the names of bidders, bid prices, and modifications or withdrawals to ensure transparency.

questions.question: What do procurement rules call a bid that fully complies with the technical, financial, and administrative requirements in the tender document?
questions.explanation: A responsive bid is one that complies fully with the technical, financial, and administrative requirements stated in the tender document. The item therefore tests recognition of the term used for a fully compliant bid.

questions.question: Which section mandates that the evaluation of bids must be based strictly on criteria and specifications stated in the solicitation documents
questions.explanation: Section 33 mandates that evaluation of bids must be based strictly on criteria stated in solicitation documents.

questions.question: After the public opening of bids, which action is expressly prohibited under the **PPA** regarding procurement documents
options: Recording the price in the minutes.
Altering or modifying the substance of the bids.
questions.explanation: Section 33 prohibits the alteration of procurement documents or bids once they have been opened.

questions.question: The standard evaluation process for goods and works typically involves how many distinct stages
options: Four stages (Initial, Mid-term, Final, and Post-audit).
questions.explanation: Bid evaluation is generally conducted in two stages: technical evaluation (compliance with specifications) and financial evaluation (price comparison).

questions.question: The final contract award recommendation must be made to the bidder who submitted the:
options: Bid with the longest execution time.
Bid from the most senior contractor.
questions.explanation: Section 33 requires awarding the contract to the lowest evaluated responsive bid to ensure value for money and compliance.

questions.question: If a contract is awarded, which **MDA** official is statutorily designated as the signatory on behalf of the procuring entity
options: The Director of Finance and Accounts.
questions.explanation: The Accounting Officer (Permanent Secretary/CEO) is designated as the contract signatory, formalizing the legal agreement.

questions.question: What is the maximum percentage of the contract sum allowable as a Mobilization Fee (advance payment) to a contractor
questions.explanation: The mobilization fee is limited to a maximum of 30% of the contract sum [22-26].

questions.question: The disbursement of the mobilization fee is conditional upon the contractor providing a(n):
questions.explanation: Mobilization fees are paid subject to the contractor providing a Performance Bond or Advance Payment Guarantee to secure the funds.

questions.question: Performance Security (or bond) is required under **Section 29** to ensure the contractor fulfills which obligation
options: To complete the works or services as specified in the contract.
questions.explanation: Section 29 requires Performance Securities to ensure contractors fulfill their contractual obligations.

questions.question: If all bids received by a procuring entity fail to meet the required technical or administrative standards, the procuring entity may invoke which measure
options: Award the contract to the highest bidder.
Reject all bids and cancel the procurement proceedings.
Negotiate secretly with the cheapest bidder.
Transfer the project to another MDA.
questions.explanation: Section 31 allows rejection of all bids if none meets the required standards or criteria, leading to cancellation of the proceeding.

questions.question: Which section requires that contract award decisions must be communicated to unsuccessful bidders
questions.explanation: Section 30 requires notifying unsuccessful bidders of the contract award decisions to ensure transparency.

questions.question: The BPP is empowered to suspend procurement proceedings if irregularities are found, as provided in:
questions.explanation: Section 53 empowers the BPP to suspend or cancel procurement if irregularities are detected, reinforcing due process.

questions.question: In the evaluation process, the technical evaluation aims to assess the bidder's:
options: Capacity, experience, and compliance with technical specifications.
questions.explanation: Technical evaluation checks the bidder’s capacity, experience, and compliance with the MDA’s detailed technical specifications.

questions.question: If a bidder fails the technical evaluation stage, they are typically excluded from the:
questions.explanation: Only technically responsive bidders proceed to the financial evaluation stage, ensuring only qualified firms compete on price.

questions.question: Which officer is typically tasked with verifying the adequacy of the Bid Security submitted by a contractor
questions.explanation: The Procurement Officer manages technical and administrative compliance, including verifying the validity and adequacy of the bid security.

questions.question: The **PPA** ensures that all bid opening procedures adhere to transparency by requiring minutes to be recorded and signed by representatives of:
options: The MDA, the BPP, and the bidders present.
questions.explanation: Bid opening minutes must be signed by the procuring entity, BPP representatives, and the bidders present to authenticate the process.

questions.question: Which section requires procuring entities to use standard bidding documents containing clear specifications and criteria?
questions.explanation: Section 25 requires standard bidding documents so bidders are assessed against clear and comparable criteria.

questions.question: A bidder who fails to submit a valid Tax Clearance Certificate (TCC) must be classified as:
questions.explanation: Failure to meet statutory eligibility criteria, such as TCC submission (Section 16), renders the bidder ineligible.

questions.question: If a bid security expires during the evaluation process, what is the required administrative action
options: The MDA must award the contract immediately.
The bidder must be automatically disqualified.
The MDA must request the bidder to extend the validity period.
The MDA must notify the FEC.
questions.explanation: The PPA generally requires bids and bid securities to remain valid throughout the process; if the validity lapses, the bidder must be requested to extend it to remain eligible.

questions.question: Contract negotiations are permitted under the PPA in which procurement situation?
options: Sole sourcing or another lawful negotiation exception.
Every Works contract above N50 million.
Any contract approved by the FEC.
questions.explanation: Negotiation is allowed only where the law creates a lawful exception to ordinary competitive bidding.

questions.question: What is the primary objective of the financial evaluation stage
options: To assess the bidder's past performance.
To verify the contractor's technical compliance.
To compare the cost-effectiveness and price of the responsive bids.
To decide on the mobilization fee.
questions.explanation: The financial evaluation compares the pricing of the technically qualified bids to determine the lowest evaluated price, supporting value for money.

questions.question: What does an abnormally low bid usually suggest during evaluation?
options: Risk of abandonment or poor-quality execution.
Too much information from the MDA.
questions.explanation: A bid that is too low may signal a risk that the bidder cannot complete the work properly.

questions.question: For a contract to be awarded, the procuring entity must first obtain the BPP's:
questions.explanation: Section 5 mandates the BPP to issue a 'Certificate of No Objection' for high-value procurements before a contract award is finalized [33-35].

questions.question: The public announcement of awarded contracts above a certain threshold is required by which section of the **PPA**'
questions.explanation: Section 32 requires the publication of contract awards above thresholds to ensure transparency.

questions.question: Within how many working days must an aggrieved bidder submit a complaint after becoming aware of the grounds for complaint
questions.explanation: Section 54 provides a complaint mechanism, requiring prompt submission of complaints to the Complaint Review Committee [35, 38-41].

questions.question: Which body is responsible for managing administrative review of procurement complaints filed by aggrieved bidders
options: The Office of the Secretary to the Government of the Federation (SGF).
questions.explanation: The Procurement Complaint Review Committee provides an administrative avenue for dispute resolution before litigation.

questions.question: If a procuring entity cancels a procurement proceeding before contract award under Section 31, what must it do next?
options: Immediately publish notice of the cancellation.
Transfer the funds to another project.
questions.explanation: Section 31 requires the entity to publish notice of the cancellation after ending the procurement proceeding.

questions.question: The use of **Pre-qualification** (**Section 22**) is primarily intended to exclude bidders who lack:
options: The required technical and professional capacity.
questions.explanation: Pre-qualification checks professional and technical capacity early to ensure only capable bidders proceed to tender Reference: Section 22).

questions.question: In the procurement of Consultancy Services, which method is typically used to ensure both competence and reasonable cost
questions.explanation: Consultancy Services (Section 43) often utilize Quality and Cost Based Selection (QCBS) to balance technical merit with price.

questions.question: Which document is required before final payment is processed to confirm satisfactory delivery of goods or services
questions.explanation: No payment is processed without the Job Completion Certificate (JCC), confirming satisfactory delivery and acceptance [12, 45-47].

questions.question: What does domestic preference under Section 35 allow?
options: A scoring advantage or margin of preference for Nigerian firms.
questions.explanation: Domestic preference may give Nigerian firms a scoring advantage or margin of preference where the law allows it.

questions.question: Which section of the **PPA** mandates that procuring entities must retain procurement documents for at least 5 years
questions.explanation: Section 38 requires procurement documents to be retained for at least 5 years to support potential audits and inquiries.

questions.question: If a bidder attempts to influence the evaluation process through bribery or collusion, penalties are prescribed under which section?
questions.explanation: Section 58 of the Public Procurement Act prescribes penalties for bribery, collusion, bid rigging, and other fraudulent or corrupt practices.

questions.question: Which entity typically verifies whether a contractor has a criminal conviction for fraud before awarding a contract
options: The Budget Office of the Federation.
The BPP and the Code of Conduct Bureau (CCB).
questions.explanation: Section 16 requires bidders to have no criminal record for fraud, verified through the BPP's database and regulatory checks.

questions.question: What happens if a contractor fails to provide the required performance bond after award?
options: Forfeiture of contract award for failure to provide the bond.
Automatic extension of the submission period.
Conversion of the bond requirement into an undertaking.
questions.explanation: The contract should not proceed if the required security has not been provided.

questions.question: What should be done when a bidder's offer is non-responsive?
options: Exclude it from further evaluation and notify the BPP.
Give the bidder 48 hours to correct the error.
questions.explanation: A non-responsive bid is excluded from further evaluation and handled according to the procurement rules.

questions.question: The requirement for using **Standard Bidding Documents (SBDs)** (**Section 25**) aims to enhance which principles
options: Standardization and equal treatment of bidders.
questions.explanation: SBDs promote uniform procedures, clarity, and ensure equal treatment of all bidders (Section 16) by standardizing required formats.

questions.question: What is the mandatory action required by the Accounting Officer upon contract award, concerning the Procurement Journal
options: The contract details must be published promptly.
The contract must be kept secret for security reasons.
The BPP must waive the publication requirement.
Only the winner’s name must be published.
questions.explanation: Section 32 requires publication of the contract award in the Procurement Journal to ensure transparency.

questions.question: Which section allows a procuring entity to reject all bids if it is determined that the prices quoted are substantially in excess of the estimated budget or market price
questions.explanation: Section 31 allows the rejection of all bids if there is insufficient competition, bids are too high, or a major irregularity is detected.

questions.question: What does post-qualification audit verify before award?
options: Continued eligibility, technical claims, and financial capacity.
questions.explanation: The audit confirms that the bidder still meets the legal, technical, and financial conditions for award.

questions.question: Which action best shows sound procurement governance in bidding, evaluation, and award?
options: Compliance with approved bidding procedures supported by complete records.
Inconsistent application of rules across similar bids.
Bypassing of review checkpoints during evaluation.
Preference for convenience over procurement compliance.
questions.explanation: Sound procurement governance in bidding and evaluation depends on following approved procedure and preserving a complete record for later review.

questions.question: For effective bidding, evaluation & award, what is the most appropriate approach to ensure bidding, evaluation & award compliance
options: Use lawful criteria and document each decision step transparently.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
questions.explanation: Use lawful criteria and document each decision step transparently. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in bidding, evaluation & award Reference: Bidding, Evaluation & Award - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which action best supports risk management in bidding, evaluation, and award?
options: Early identification of control gaps with prompt escalation of material exceptions.
questions.explanation: Risk management starts with early detection of control gaps and prompt escalation of exceptions.

questions.question: When handling bidding, evaluation & award, which choice reflects proper open competition standards
options: Use competitive procurement methods except where lawful exceptions apply.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
questions.explanation: Use competitive procurement methods except where lawful exceptions apply. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in bidding, evaluation & award Reference: Bidding, Evaluation & Award - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice should a responsible officer prioritize to sustain bid evaluation in Bidding, Evaluation & Award
options: Apply published criteria consistently to all responsive bids.
Prioritize convenience over policy and legal requirements.
Ignore feedback and continue non-compliant procedures.
Apply rules inconsistently based on personal preference.
questions.explanation: Apply published criteria consistently to all responsive bids. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in bidding, evaluation & award Reference: Bidding, Evaluation & Award - Expansion Set.

questions.question: In the context of Bidding, Evaluation & Award, which action best demonstrates contract governance
options: Monitor delivery milestones and enforce contract obligations.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
questions.explanation: Monitor delivery milestones and enforce contract obligations. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in bidding, evaluation & award Reference: Bidding, Evaluation & Award - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice best supports procurement ethics in bidding, evaluation, and award?
options: Prevention of collusion, favoritism, and conflicts of interest.
Inconsistent application of rules across similar bids.
Bypassing of review checkpoints during evaluation.
Preference for convenience over procurement compliance.
questions.explanation: Procurement ethics in bidding and evaluation depend on preventing collusion, favoritism, and conflicts of interest throughout the process.

questions.question: Which option most strongly aligns with good public-service practice on regulatory compliance within Bidding, Evaluation & Award
options: Obtain required approvals before award and contract execution.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
questions.explanation: Obtain required approvals before award and contract execution. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in bidding, evaluation & award Reference: Bidding, Evaluation & Award - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice best supports documented procedure during evaluation and award?
options: Recording of each decision step with preserved file evidence.
Inconsistent application of rules across similar bids.
Bypassing of review checkpoints during evaluation.
Preference for convenience over procurement compliance.
questions.explanation: Documented procedure in evaluation and award requires each decision step to be recorded and the supporting file evidence to be preserved.

questions.question: Which practice should a responsible officer prioritize to sustain compliance assurance in Bidding, Evaluation & Award
options: Apply approved rules consistently and escalate exceptions.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
questions.explanation: Apply approved rules consistently and escalate exceptions. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in bidding, evaluation & award Reference: Bidding, Evaluation & Award - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which action best demonstrates public accountability in bidding, evaluation, and award?
options: Traceable decisions supported by evidence-based justification.
Bypassing of review checkpoints during evaluation.
Preference for convenience over procurement compliance.
questions.explanation: Public accountability in bidding and award depends on decisions that are traceable and justified by evidence rather than convenience.

questions.question: For effective bidding, evaluation & award, what is the most appropriate approach to ensure service integrity
options: Avoid conflicts of interest and disclose relevant constraints.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
questions.explanation: Avoid conflicts of interest and disclose relevant constraints. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in bidding, evaluation & award Reference: Bidding, Evaluation & Award - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice best supports risk control in bidding, evaluation, and award?
options: Early risk identification, control application, and mitigation documentation.
questions.explanation: Risk control means identifying risks early, applying controls, and documenting mitigation.

questions.question: When handling bidding, evaluation & award, which choice reflects proper decision transparency standards
options: Use clear criteria and communicate decisions promptly.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
questions.explanation: Use clear criteria and communicate decisions promptly. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in bidding, evaluation & award Reference: Bidding, Evaluation & Award - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice best sustains operational discipline in bidding, evaluation, and award?
options: Approved workflows with verification of outputs before closure.
questions.explanation: Operational discipline means following the approved workflow and verifying the output before closure.

questions.question: In the context of Bidding, Evaluation & Award, which action best demonstrates citizen-focused service
options: Balance legality, fairness, timeliness, and service quality.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
questions.explanation: Balance legality, fairness, timeliness, and service quality. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in bidding, evaluation & award Reference: Bidding, Evaluation & Award - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice best supports record management in bidding, evaluation, and award?
options: Accurate files with status updates at each control point.
questions.explanation: Record management depends on accurate files and status updates at each stage.

questions.question: Which option most strongly aligns with good public-service practice on performance standards within Bidding, Evaluation & Award
options: Set measurable targets, monitor progress, and correct deviations.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
questions.explanation: Set measurable targets, monitor progress, and correct deviations. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in bidding, evaluation & award Reference: Bidding, Evaluation & Award - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which action best demonstrates bidding, evaluation, and award governance?
options: Maintenance of an auditable decision trail.
Bypassing of review checkpoints during evaluation.
Preference for convenience over procurement compliance.
questions.explanation: Governance in bidding and award is strengthened by an auditable decision trail showing how each step was reviewed and decided.

questions.question: Which practice should a responsible officer prioritize to sustain bidding, evaluation & award compliance in Bidding, Evaluation & Award
options: Use lawful criteria and document each decision step transparently.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
questions.explanation: Use lawful criteria and document each decision step transparently. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in bidding, evaluation & award Reference: Bidding, Evaluation & Award - Expansion Set.

questions.question: In the context of Bidding, Evaluation & Award, which action best demonstrates bidding, evaluation & award risk management
options: Identify control gaps early and escalate material exceptions promptly.
Prioritize convenience over policy and legal requirements.
Ignore feedback and continue non-compliant procedures.
Apply rules inconsistently based on personal preference.
questions.explanation: Identify control gaps early and escalate material exceptions promptly. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in bidding, evaluation & award Reference: Bidding, Evaluation & Award - Expansion Set.

questions.question: For effective bidding, evaluation & award, what is the most appropriate approach to ensure open competition
options: Use competitive procurement methods except where lawful exceptions apply.
Close cases without validating facts or required records.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
questions.explanation: Use competitive procurement methods except where lawful exceptions apply. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in bidding, evaluation & award Reference: Bidding, Evaluation & Award - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice best supports fair bid evaluation?
options: Consistent application of published criteria to all responsive bids.
questions.explanation: Fairness requires the published criteria to be applied consistently to every responsive bid.

questions.question: When handling bidding, evaluation & award, which choice reflects proper contract governance standards
options: Monitor delivery milestones and enforce contract obligations.
Rely on informal instructions without documentary evidence.
Delay decisions until issues escalate into avoidable crises.
Treat exceptions as routine without documented justification.
questions.explanation: Monitor delivery milestones and enforce contract obligations. It strengthens compliance, consistency, and accountability in bidding, evaluation & award Reference: Bidding, Evaluation & Award - Expansion Set.

questions.question: Which practice best sustains procurement ethics in bidding, evaluation, and award?
options: Disclosure of conflicts with preservation of impartiality.
Inconsistent application of rules across similar bids.
Bypassing of review checkpoints during evaluation.
Preference for convenience over procurement compliance.
questions.explanation: Procurement ethics are sustained when conflicts are disclosed and impartiality is preserved throughout bidding and evaluation.

questions.question: Which of the following was one limitation of the 1988 Civil Service Reforms?
options: Strong commitment from top government functionaries.
Inadequate knowledge of the reform provisions among Civil Servants and senior officials.
questions.explanation: One limitation of the 1988 Civil Service Reforms was that many Civil Servants and top government functionaries lacked adequate knowledge of the reform provisions.

questions.question: What was a major limitation of the 1988 Civil Service Reforms?
options: Top government functionaries lacked strong commitment because they feared losing power.
The reforms were too simple to implement.
The reforms received too much funding.
The reforms were too popular with the public.
questions.explanation: A major limitation was the weak commitment of top government functionaries, who feared the reforms would reduce their powers.

questions.question: If a public officer performs a prohibited act through a nominee, trustee, or agent, how is that conduct treated under the Code?
options: It is treated as a breach of the Code.
It is ignored because the act was not done personally.
It is treated as fulfillment of duty.
It is treated as lawful conduct.
questions.explanation: A public officer who performs a prohibited act through a nominee, trustee, or agent is still treated as having committed a breach of the Code.

questions.question: Under FR 1802, what is the minimum membership of a Board of Survey?
questions.explanation: A Board of Survey must have at least three members under FR 1802.

questions.question: What should you do if papers or documents are sent to you by mistake from staff or members of the public?
options: Find the correct destination and obtain direction from your superior on how to re-route them.
Take them to the wrong office.
questions.explanation: If papers or documents come to you by mistake, you should identify the correct destination and obtain direction from your superior on how to re-route them properly.

questions.question: Which file contains correspondence and documents relating to a particular staff member?
options: The official history of a ministry.
Documents on a particular subject matter.
Correspondence and documents relating to a particular staff member.
questions.explanation: A personal file contains correspondence and related documents concerning a particular staff member rather than ministry-wide or subject-wide records.

questions.question: Which option correctly defines the objective of a register of correspondences?
options: To list all the staff in a Ministry.
To list all the correspondences received and sent out by a Ministry.
questions.explanation: A register of correspondences tracks the letters and other correspondence received and sent by the Ministry.

questions.question: What is the secretary's role during a meeting?
options: To act as the official recorder of all discussions, decisions, and action points.
To take charge of all financial matters.
questions.explanation: The secretary serves as the official recorder of the meeting's discussions, decisions, and action points.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the minutes of a meeting?
options: To record the private lives of members.
To summarize only the chairman's personal opinions.
To list only the names of members present.
To serve as the official record of the proceedings and decisions of the meeting.
questions.explanation: Minutes serve as the official record of the proceedings and decisions of a meeting so that actions and resolutions can be traced afterward.

questions.question: What word is used to route minutes through official channels?
questions.explanation: The style of address for minutes uses the word through before the officer list.

questions.question: Which principles are strengthened by the use of Standard Bidding Documents under Section 25?
options: Standardization and equal treatment of bidders.
questions.explanation: Standard Bidding Documents promote standardization, comparability, and equal treatment because all bidders respond to the same requirements.

questions.question: If a bid security expires during the evaluation process, what should the procuring entity do?
options: Request the bidder to extend the validity period.
Notify the Federal Executive Council immediately.
questions.explanation: The procuring entity should request an extension so the bid security remains valid until evaluation is concluded.

questions.question: Which section deals with the requirement to use standard bidding documents with clear specifications and criteria?
questions.explanation: Section 25 requires standard bidding documents with clear specifications and criteria for bidders.

questions.question: The award of a contract following evaluation is required by **Section 33** to be granted to the:
options: Bidder with the highest technical score.
Bidder who offers the largest discount.
questions.explanation: Section 33 requires awarding the contract to the lowest evaluated responsive bid.

questions.question: Under the Public Procurement Act, what bidding method should normally be used for public procurement?
questions.explanation: The Public Procurement Act provides that public procurement should normally be conducted through open competitive bidding.

subcategories.name: Transparency, Ethics & Accountability
subcategories.description: Ethics, transparency, and anti-corruption controls in procurement.

questions.question: Which section of the **PPA** 2007 prohibits conflict of interest and mandates disclosure by officials
questions.explanation: Section 57 strictly prohibits conflict of interest in procurement proceedings and requires mandatory disclosure by procurement officials.

questions.question: The intentional act of fixing or manipulating bid outcomes to restrict competition is known as collusion or bid rigging. Under which section is it prohibited?
questions.explanation: Section 58 prohibits collusion, bid rigging, and other fraudulent or corrupt practices in public procurement.

questions.question: What is the consequence for a procurement officer found guilty of an offense under **Section 58** (e.g., fraud or bid rigging)
questions.explanation: Section 58 prescribes penalties, including imprisonment and fines, for procurement-related fraud and breaches by officials and contractors.

questions.question: Which section of the **PPA** 2007 mandates that all procurement decisions must be properly documented and recorded
questions.explanation: Section 34 requires that all procurement decisions, including reasons for the chosen method and rejection of bids, must be properly documented.

questions.question: Procurement documents and records must be retained by the procuring entity for a minimum period of:
questions.explanation: Section 38 requires that all procurement documents be retained for a minimum of 5 years to facilitate audit and inspection.

questions.question: The **PPA** principle of accountability is supported by the requirement to publish all contracts awarded above a specific threshold in the:
questions.explanation: Section 12 mandates the establishment and publication of procurement journals to ensure wide dissemination of information regarding contracts awarded, supporting transparency.

questions.question: The BPP is statutorily empowered to blacklist or permanently exclude contractors who commit offenses under **Section 58**. This is based on sanctions provided in:
questions.explanation: Section 57 empowers the BPP to blacklist bidders for engaging in corrupt practices.

questions.question: The principle of transparency in bid opening (**Section 27**) requires the procurement entity to record and sign the minutes in the presence of:
options: Only the Accounting Officer and the Minister.
The BPP representative and the bidders present.
The Head of Civil Service and the Auditor-General.
questions.explanation: To ensure fairness, bid opening minutes must be authenticated by the Procuring Entity, BPP (or relevant body), and the bidders present Reference: Section 27).

questions.question: Which section enshrines the principle that all bidders must be treated without prejudice or discrimination throughout the procurement process?
questions.explanation: Section 16 sets out the principles of procurement, including the requirement for fair and equal treatment of all bidders.

questions.question: Why is the Accounting Officer (Permanent Secretary) ultimately responsible for compliance, financial integrity, and accountability in procurement?
options: Designation as the Chief Accounting Officer for the MDA.
Personal authorship of all procurement documents.
Exclusive signing of all payment vouchers.
questions.explanation: The Permanent Secretary or equivalent is the Chief Accounting Officer and therefore carries ultimate responsibility for expenditure control and compliance.

questions.question: An officer involved in procurement who receives confidential information and uses it for personal financial advantage violates which ethical mandate
questions.explanation: Using official information for personal gain is strictly prohibited, falling under the conflict of interest rule (Section 57 PPA and PSR Chapter 15).

questions.question: The system put in place to ensure integrity and traceability of all financial decisions and documentation throughout the procurement cycle is called the:
questions.explanation: The Audit Trail provides a chronological record of transactions, allowing auditors to verify integrity and traceability, ensuring accountability.

questions.question: **Section 58** explicitly forbids the illegal breaking down of contracts to avoid higher authority approval, a practice termed:
questions.explanation: Contract splitting is a form of procurement fraud prohibited under Section 58(4), designed to circumvent statutory approval thresholds.

questions.question: Which body is responsible for conducting procurement audits of **MDAs** to ensure compliance with the **PPA**'
questions.explanation: Section 6 empowers the BPP to conduct procurement audits of procuring entities to monitor compliance.

questions.question: An officer who fails to report an offer of bribe or inducement immediately to the appropriate authority violates ethical standards under which **PSR** chapter?
questions.explanation: Chapter 15 emphasizes integrity and requires public officers to reject and promptly report offers of bribe or inducement to the appropriate authority.

questions.question: The legal action that enables the government to recover the exact amount of financial loss caused by an officer’s negligence or misconduct is known as:
questions.explanation: Surcharge is the recovery of government losses from the officer's pay, applied when the loss is due to the officer’s fault.

questions.question: What is the consequence for an officer who fails the Confirmation Examination after three consecutive attempts
options: Required to resign or be withdrawn from the service.
questions.explanation: Rule 030104 states that an Officer who fails the confirmation examination after three consecutive attempts shall be required to resign or be withdrawn from the service.

questions.question: The principle of **accountability** in procurement is primarily supported by which BPP practice after bid opening
questions.explanation: The Post-Qualification Audit involves reviewing the bidder’s documentation to verify their continued technical and financial eligibility *after* bid opening, ensuring compliance.

questions.question: The body responsible for monitoring compliance with anti-corruption laws and enforcing ethical standards in public finance (excluding financial crime prosecution) is the:
questions.explanation: The ICPC monitors compliance with anti-corruption laws and investigates institutional corruption and ethical breaches [26-28].

questions.question: An officer under **Interdiction** receives:
questions.explanation: During interdiction (temporary suspension pending investigation), an officer is placed on half salary (Rule 020412).

questions.question: Which document must be published promptly to fulfill the transparency requirement regarding contract awards
questions.explanation: The publication of contract details in the Procurement Journal (Section 12/32) ensures transparency after award.

questions.question: How do Standard Bidding Documents (SBDs) support transparency?
options: They allow only local contractors to bid.
By ensuring uniformity and clarity of requirements for all potential bidders.
They make negotiation mandatory for all bids.
questions.explanation: Standard Bidding Documents promote transparency by giving all bidders the same clear requirements and evaluation basis.

questions.question: Which specific **PSR** Chapter provides the foundation for integrity, neutrality, and accountability in public service conduct
questions.explanation: Chapter 15 of the PSR (or Chapter 10 of PSR 2021 cited in the source) deals specifically with Public Service Conduct and Ethics, emphasizing integrity and accountability.

questions.question: Misconduct involving the Falsification of Records is classified under the **PSR** as a:
questions.explanation: Falsification of records, along with fraud and corruption, constitutes Serious Misconduct, potentially leading to dismissal (Rule 100401).

questions.question: What is the mandatory frequency for an officer to declare their assets under the Code of Conduct for Public Officers
options: Every four years, and upon assumption of duty.
questions.explanation: Asset declaration is required upon assumption of duty and every four years thereafter, as mandated by the Fifth Schedule of the Constitution [37-40].

questions.question: Which agency is primarily responsible for enforcing the asset declaration rules across the public service
questions.explanation: The Code of Conduct Bureau (CCB) receives and verifies public officers’ asset declarations Reference: rules.

questions.question: The loss of government funds or property must be communicated immediately to which three offices (among others) for proper fiscal accountability and investigation
options: Permanent Secretary, Legal Department, and Revenue Collector.
questions.explanation: Mandatory loss reporting requires notification to the Police, Accountant-General, and Auditor-General for criminal and fiscal accountability.

questions.question: The **PPA** principle of **transparency** directly supports the constitutional right of citizens to access information, as formalized by the:
questions.explanation: The PPA's transparency mandates complement the FOI Act, which grants citizens the right to request public records.

questions.question: Who has the final authority to discipline Directors (**GL 17**) in the Federal Civil Service
options: The Head of the Civil Service of the Federation (OHCSF).
questions.explanation: The Civil Service Commission is the disciplinary authority for Directors (GL 17) and officers on GL 07 and above [45-47].

questions.question: Which section provides penalties for procurement fraud, collusion, and misrepresentation
questions.explanation: Section 58 covers procurement offenses, ensuring penalties for fraudulent and corrupt practices [3-6, 48].

questions.question: What is the penalty for an officer who engages in political activities while in service
options: A transfer to a political post.
questions.explanation: Public officers are prohibited from engaging in any political activities; violations lead to disciplinary action (PSR Chapter 10).

questions.question: The requirement that all funds must be checked in the Vote Book before commitment is made serves as an internal control against:
questions.explanation: Checking the Vote Book (Expenditure Ledger) is mandatory to ensure the remaining balance is adequate, serving as a first line of defense against Over-Expenditure.

questions.question: When an **MDA** cancels a procurement proceeding due to irregularities (**Section 31**), which administrative step must follow promptly to maintain transparency
options: The Accounting Officer must be interdicted.
The MDA must publish a notice of the cancellation.
The BPP must immediately award the contract to the next bidder.
questions.explanation: Section 31 requires the procuring entity to publish a notice of the cancellation immediately to ensure transparency and accountability.

questions.question: Which agency is responsible for investigating and prosecuting financial crimes, fraud, and money laundering related to public funds
questions.explanation: The EFCC investigates and prosecutes financial crimes, fraud, and money laundering [26, 53-55].

questions.question: The segregation of duties—where no single officer controls all three functions of approval, custody, and recording—is a fundamental control measure against:
questions.explanation: Segregation of Duties is the primary internal control measure used to minimize fraud risk by separating approval, custody, and record-keeping roles.

questions.question: The penalty of **Dismissal** for serious misconduct results in:
options: Forfeiture of all claims to retirement benefits.
Demotion to the next lower grade level.
questions.explanation: Dismissal is the ultimate disciplinary penalty (Rule 100401) and leads to the permanent removal from service and forfeiture of most benefits.

questions.question: Which section requires the Accounting Officer to take into account the gravity of the shortcoming and the quality of the officer's previous service when deciding whether to withhold or defer an increment
